summary: DHH explains his long-standing aversion to angel investing, rooted in distaste for the self-aggrandizing 'angel investor' label and the unicorn-obsessed startup culture. He acknowledges that while bootstrapping worked for Basecamp, not every entrepreneur can self-fund, and some non-builder types bring valuable skills like product management and sales. He's softened his stance enough to make small investments in Danish software companies, specifically ones open to sustainable growth rather than chasing venture capital and unicorn exits. He frames this as breeding 'zebras' instead of unicorns — profitable, grounded businesses that serve their communities.
key_points: The 'angel investor' persona is self-serving theater, often masking luck and connections as visionary skill
Not all entrepreneurs can bootstrap — some need modest funding to get started, especially non-builders with strong product or sales skills
If the only available funding comes from VC-pipeline angels, an entire class of sustainable businesses will never get the chance to exist
DHH is investing in Danish software companies that are open to unicorn-free growth plans, steering them away from the default VC route
Most businesses fail regardless of funding model — invest only what you're willing to lose
money_quotes: A winged hustler chasing mythical creatures with a dull trident, blind-folded. Occasionally nailing the throw, usually out of luck, connections, or happy timing, then heralding themselves as visionaries, demanding a parade.
While we bootstrapped Basecamp to the moon, not everyone has the capacity to build the thrusters needed in a shed behind an existing business.

Just as the world is full of bozo business types who think they're owed millions because they can dazzle a powerpoint with all the right keywords, it's also full of talented builder types who couldn't come up with a compelling product or find a way to sell it if their life depended on it.
We desperately need more tech companies that aren't solely focused on unicorn stardom, the big exit, or any of the other bullshit markers of success in that world.

If the only source of funding that's available to these entrepreneurs come from angels who are hoping to float the business down the stream to venture capitalists, we're never going to see whether this class of businesses might prosper outside that playpen.
All I had to do was tickle that hesitation and reservation... and of course offer an alternative (albeit significantly smaller) bag of money.
Even if you're aiming to breed zebras, most businesses fail. Only invest what you're willing to lose.
key_insight: The tech startup ecosystem needs funding options beyond VC-pipeline angel investing so that sustainable, non-unicorn businesses have a viable path to existence.
